retrieved_docs.title: Preventing Cross Infection in the Dental Office -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Postexposure management for hepatitis B virus (HBV) involves providing multiple doses of hepatitis B immune globulin and the hepatitis B vaccine. [14] Hepatitis B immunoglobulin is a human immunoglobulin used to prevent the development of hepatitis B after acute exposure to the surface antigen of the hepatitis B virus (HBsAg). [15] The level of antibodies of the exposed individual should be tested if the source is positive for HBsAg. If the exposed person is not immune (has never been immunized, did not seroconvert to the vaccine, or has antibody levels to HBsAg less than 10 mIU/mL), the treatment is to give a single dose of hepatitis B immunoglobulin between 48 to 72 hours of the exposure and to start a course of HBV vaccination. [14] The HBV vaccine should be given within 7 days of exposure, 1 or 2 months after, and then 6 months after the first dose. The level of immunity should be tested 2 to 4 weeks later. The risk of transmission of HBV is more than 30% if the source is positive and post-exposure prophylaxis has not been used. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Preventing Cross Infection in the Dental Office -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Dental healthcare workers are at risk of common vaccine-preventable infections. The goal of immunization is to reduce the risk of acquiring a disease. All clinical personnel must receive immunization against hepatitis B within 10 days of patient contact. [2] They are allowed to see patients when needed to complete the vaccination scheme, usually between 2 and 6 months. [2] Immunization is achieved when the hepatitis B surface antibody (HbsAb) is more than 100 mIU/ml. A poor responder is defined as having antibody levels between 10 and 100 mIU/ml, while levels below 10 mIU/ml characterize a non-responder. Furthermore, immunization for common vaccine-preventable conditions is strongly recommended, including: [9] Varicella (if seronegative). Measles, mumps, and rubella (MMR) (if non-immune). Pertussis (whooping cough). Viral influenza (required every year to cover new) [8] Personal protective equipment

retrieved_docs.title: Mumps (Nursing) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical observation and laboratory testing confirm a mumps infection. Not all mumps cases classically display orchitis and parotitis and individuals may present heterogeneously. During an outbreak, the diagnosis is clinical in cases of parotid swelling with a history of exposure. When the local incidence is low other causes of parotitis warrant investigation. Laboratory testing is not routinely necessary to confirm a mumps viral infection, but in equivocal cases testing for other viral infections such as HIV, influenza, and para-influenza is necessary. Staphylococcus aureus is not an uncommon cause of suppurative parotitis. Recurrent parotid swelling of unknown etiology warrants an investigation for ductal calculi and malignancy. A viral mumps infection in the absence of parotid swelling and/or salivary gland involvement may present with symptoms of visceral and CNS predominance. In these cases, diagnosis relies on positive antibody titers and virus culture from oral secretions, urine, blood, and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF). Laboratory confirmation techniques include reverse transcriptase polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) and serum IgM antibodies. RT-PCR is for serum and oral secretions. RT-PCR specimens additionally are for viral cultures. At the initial presentation of an individual suspected of mumps infection collect 2 specimens: a buccal or oral swab for RT-PCR and also an acute phase serum specimen for IgM antibody, IgG antibody, and serum viral RT-PCR. Obtain oral specimens within three days of parotid gland swelling and no later than 8 days after the start of symptoms. The IgM response may not be detectable for up to five days after the onset of symptoms. Incorrect collection of acute-phase samples lead to false-negative IgM and RT-PCR tests. When this occurs collect repeat samples 5 to 10 days after symptom onset to yield positive results. Laboratory confirmation of an acute viral infection in individuals with prior vaccination is difficult. This occurs due to multiple reasons: IgM antibodies are negative in a large number of patients, and RT-PCR results may be falsely negative. The disease is a Center for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) reportable illness with most states requiring reporting in 1 to 3 days. [21] [22] [23] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: All IgA deficient patines, even if asymptomatic, should receive polyvalent pneumococcal and influenza vaccines; patients must avoid attenuated or live vaccines. Specifically, live vaccines such as oral polio, Bacille-Calmette-Guerin, and yellow fever, should be considered contraindicated due to the threat of subsequent severe infection.

retrieved_docs.title: X-Linked Agammaglobulinemia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: There is no curative treatment for XLA. However, management is by preventing, reducing, and treating infections. [3] [1] The optimal management of patients with XLA includes [21] [22] [23] [24] [23] [22] : Regular immunoglobulin replacement therapy, using intravenous or subcutaneous infusions Therapeutic and prophylactic use of antibiotics to treat and prevent bacterial infections Careful monitoring to manage reactions arising from immunoglobulin infusions, complications of infections, or the emergence of clinical disease (e.g., autoimmune, inflammatory, malignant) Support (nutritional, social, psychological, and educational) Counseling about the importance of receiving all available immunizations except for those containing live bacteria or viruses, e.g., polio (OPV, oral polio vaccine), measles/mumps/rubella (MMR), chickenpox (Varivax), BCG, yellow fever, and rotavirus (Rota-Teq)

retrieved_docs.title: Agammaglobulinemia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: These patients should not receive live vaccines such as oral polio or MMR. Because of the deficient immunoglobulins, they are not capable of mounting the appropriate immunological response to these vaccines. There are reported cases of patients with XLA that developed vaccine-related poliomyelitis after vaccination with live attenuated poliovirus. The condition carries a high rate of mortality, and those who survived had severe neurological complications, such as permanent paralysis. Hence, special emphasis is given to avoiding the oral live attenuated SABIN-type polio vaccine. Furthermore, it is not proven that active vaccines, in general, have any beneficial effect on patients with XLA, as they lack the normal ability to maintain immune memory.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Caries Diagnostic Testing -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Dental caries remains one of the most widespread chronic infections globally. Fortunately, numerous diagnostic tests developed over the past century now enable earlier detection of the disease, supporting timely intervention and more effective management to address caries before significant progression occurs.

retrieved_docs.title: Common Variable Immunodeficiency -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with CVID require an integrated interprofessional team approach to improve short-term and long-term patient outcomes. They need frequent specialized clinical provider evaluations, including an infectious disease specialist and immunologist. A dedicated nurse can assess and educate the patient as well as his family about potential complications and symptoms to monitor. A specialized pharmacist is required to ensure proper dosing of IVIG as well as appropriate dosing and duration of antibiotics for infection episodes. Above all, the clinical provider, the nursing staff, as well as the clinical pharmacist, need to provide an integrated interprofessional approach to patient education to minimize complications of the disease and to improve patient outcomes. Preventive care with appropriate vaccination (avoiding live vaccines such as oral polio, smallpox, influenza, yellow fever, oral typhoid), dental, auditive, and mental care requires attention, as mentioned previously. Close communication between the team members is vital to ensure good outcomes. [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: X-Linked Agammaglobulinemia -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patients and their families should be provided resources and educational materials informing them about vaccines, immunoglobulin replacement therapy, prophylactic and therapeutic use of antibiotics, routine and emergency medical care, the importance of keeping follow-up appointments, healthcare team specialists, preparation for traveling, the importance of record-keeping, support, and how to become a good patient advocate. Reasons for avoiding live virus vaccines, such as oral polio, mumps, measles, rubella, rotavirus, yellow fever, chickenpox, should be explained thoroughly. Patients and families should be given the following advice when planning a trip: Carry a portable water filtration system, prophylactic antibiotics, and other medications; Know the vaccination requirements for traveling to foreign countries; be prepared to comply or obtain formal exemptions; Carry an approved list of resources, including the locations, contact information, hours of operation, for:

retrieved_docs.title: Corynebacterium Diphtheriae -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Socioeconomic factors, low-income status, unemployment, cultural and religious beliefs, false perceptions about vaccinations, educational status of mothers, premature birth, and parental absent-mindedness are some of the hindrances that affect the immunization status of an individual and, in turn increasing the likelihood of infection with a toxigenic strain of C. diphtheria . Certain health conditions, including dental caries, a heart condition, diabetes mellitus, are associated with increased risk for infection with a non-toxigenic strain of C. diphtheria . Among these, intravenous drug or alcohol abuse and homelessness are major risk factors for contracting the disease. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1459
retrieved_docs.content: Microbiology is identified as an applicable qualifier for the oral conditions قيلة مثانية, قيلة مستقيمية, and اضطراب اللسان, indicating that microbiological factors are relevant in the assessment or management of these disorders. Bacterial vaccines are, in turn, an applicable qualifier for microbiology, reflecting the role of immunization strategies within the broader microbiological context. The term “Without” is noted as not being associated with either قيلة مثانية or قيلة مستقيمية, suggesting that the absence of a particular factor does not have a direct relationship with these specific conditions. Together, these connections highlight the importance of microbiological considerations and vaccine‑related interventions in certain oral pathologies, while clarifying that “Without” does not contribute to their etiology or presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1591
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease encompasses human vaccination as a specific form of immunization, indicating that vaccines administered to humans can be used to prevent or modulate autoimmune conditions. Human vaccination is situated within the broader category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, reflecting its role as a preventive intervention designed to protect individuals from infectious agents. Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations functions as the overarching classification that groups together all preventive vaccine strategies, including human vaccination, and thereby provides the hierarchical framework linking general preventive immunization concepts to the more specific application of immunization for autoimmune disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2570
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease is organized hierarchically to include human vaccination, and human vaccination in turn encompasses التمنيع السلبي (passive immunization). Injection of RhD immune globulin represents a specific form of التمنيع السلبي, illustrating that this RhD immune‑globulin injection is an example of passive immunization used within the broader context of human vaccination strategies for autoimmune disease prevention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2184
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease includes human vaccination as a subordinate concept, indicating that human vaccination is regarded as a specific form of immunization employed to prevent or modulate autoimmune disease in humans.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2290
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease is administered through the injection or infusion of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and is classified as a child procedure within the broader category of such therapeutic injections or infusions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 896
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease includes human vaccination as a specific child category, indicating that human vaccination is a form of immunization directed toward autoimmune conditions. Human vaccination is also placed under the broader class of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, reflecting its role in preventive health measures. Within human vaccination, there is a subcategory called التمنيع السلبي, which represents a particular type of negative immunization. Injection of RhD immune globulin is linked to التمنيع السلبي through an inverse hierarchical relationship, suggesting that this injection is a specific instance encompassed by the broader concept of التمنيع السلبي. The category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations classifies itself, emphasizing its function as a primary classification for preventive immunization strategies. Together, these relationships illustrate a structured taxonomy of vaccination and immunization practices, from general preventive categories to specific interventions such as RhD immune globulin, aiding comprehensive understanding and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1759
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is classified as a therapeutic intervention and is a subcategory of the broader group termed Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This relationship indicates that preventive vaccination against bacterial pathogens is considered part of the overall therapeutic strategies that include various forms of immunization, injectable treatments, and perfusion techniques, highlighting its role within comprehensive patient care aimed at preventing infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 881
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations represent a broad category of therapeutic interventions that encompass immunization, injections, and perfusion. Within this overarching group, human vaccination is a specific subclass, and it further includes distinct forms such as immunization for autoimmune disease and passive immunization (التمنيع السلبي). Passive immunization, in turn, comprises interventions like the injection of RhD immune globulin, which is recognized as a type of passive immunization. Additionally, prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is classified both as a therapeutic intervention (under immunization, injections, and perfusion) and as a component of the larger prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations category. This hierarchical organization highlights how various vaccination and immunization strategies are interrelated, ranging from general preventive measures to specialized applications for autoimmune conditions and passive immune protection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: disorders are placed under the broader category of blood‑globulin deficiency (نقص غلوبيولين الدم), indicating that globulin deficiency underlies or is related to various immune system abnormalities. Recognizing malaise, fatigue, and the link between immunity disorders and globulin deficiency, together with the shared pharmacologic strategies and contraindications, supports an integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approach in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2235
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of RhD immune globulin is classified as a form of التمنيع السلبي (passive immunization). Within the broader framework of human vaccination, التمنيع السلبي represents a child category, indicating that passive immunization is considered a subset of vaccination strategies applied to humans. Consequently, the administration of RhD immune globulin exemplifies how passive immunization techniques are incorporated into human vaccination programs to provide immediate, short‑term protection, particularly in contexts such as preventing hemolytic disease of the newborn. This relationship underscores the role of passive immunization as an integral component of comprehensive vaccination approaches in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2568
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is identified as a therapeutic intervention that includes immunization, injections, and perfusion methods. This specific preventive measure is placed under the broader category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, indicating that it has a parent relationship to that general class. The overarching class of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations functions to classify and organize these preventive immunization strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2351
retrieved_docs.content: Vibrio cholerae, the gram‑negative bacterium that causes cholera, is directly associated with cholera vaccines, which are formulated to induce immunity against this pathogen and thereby prevent the severe diarrheal disease it produces. Understanding the link between Vibrio cholerae and cholera vaccines is essential for developing effective immunization strategies, guiding public‑health interventions, and reducing the incidence of cholera outbreaks worldwide.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2100
retrieved_docs.content: Injection or infusion of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases are both classified under the broader category of Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This parent category encompasses medical procedures that deliver therapeutic agents directly into the body, either through injection or infusion, as well as preventive measures such as bacterial vaccinations. By grouping these specific interventions within the same therapeutic framework, clinicians recognize that both the administration of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and the use of bacterial vaccines are integral components of immunization and perfusion strategies aimed at preventing disease and delivering treatment effectively.
